summary: Gruber dismantles Mike Daisey's defense after being exposed for fabricating key details in his theatrical monologue about Apple's Chinese factory conditions. Daisey claimed his fabrications served a 'larger truth,' but Gruber argues they instead constituted a 'big lie' that painted Apple as callously indifferent to child labor and worker safety. Gruber methodically shows that the actual problems—underage workers, n-hexane poisoning, suicides—had already been reported by Apple itself, the New York Times, and ABC News. The critical difference was that Daisey claimed these problems were so rampant that a casual visitor could witness them all in a single trip, which no other reporter corroborated. Gruber concludes that Daisey's fabrications actively hurt the cause of labor reform by drawing attention to nonexistent problems at the expense of real ones.
key_points: Daisey's defense—that fabricated details served a 'larger truth'—is itself dishonest because his story wasn't about known problems but about Apple's supposed callous indifference
Every verified labor issue in Apple's supply chain had already been reported by Apple itself, the New York Times, and ABC News before Daisey's show
Daisey's specific lie was that problems were so pervasive any visitor could encounter child workers, poisoned employees, and workplace deaths in a single six-day trip
The Change.org petition with 250,000 signatures was based on claims—like epidemic repetitive stress injuries—that only Daisey reported and fabricated
Daisey's fabrications drew attention away from real labor issues and toward fictional ones, actively harming the cause he claimed to champion
money_quotes: Daisey was not getting at a larger truth. He was instead painting a big lie.
No one, as a result of Daisey being outed as a liar, is now looking to ignore labor conditions in Chinese factories.
There is no larger truth here. This is not a mistake. This is simply a lie, a lie that was told to draw attention and create sympathy at the expense of the actual truth.
This is about a big lie: that Apple as an institution, and its executives personally, have a callous disregard for the welfare of children and are either lying to us or looking the other way.

The actual larger truth — underage workers, unsafe conditions, grueling hours, crowded dormitories — are all real problems, and all deserve our attention. But that's exactly what Apple itself has been saying for five years.

Daisey impugned the integrity of Apple — and the journalism of ABC News — in order to work people up regarding problems that don't exist. This only served to draw attention away from the labor, health, and environmental issues in Apple's Asian supply chain that do exist.
He has hurt the true cause, not helped it.
I think what Daisey really believes is that he deserves the attention his lies have brought him, and so he does not intend to stop telling them.
key_insight: Fabricating sensational details about real problems doesn't illuminate a larger truth—it obscures the actual truth and undermines the credibility of legitimate reporting on those very problems.
